html:
Nuix is an orphaned Australian software turnaround hiding inside a scandal discount. The old label is failed 2020 IPO, legal overhang, lumpy license revenue, and management churn. The current business is a forensic data engine with 96% subscription ACV, improving cash conversion, a new CEO with enterprise software experience, and a credible path to becoming the regulated-industry data layer for AI-assisted investigations.

The market is still pricing the scar, not the forward model. At about €0.86 for 3SJ.F, equivalent to roughly A$1.39 on ASX:NXL, Nuix is worth about A$470-490 million. After the Linkurious close, net cash should still be positive but lower, roughly A$25-35 million, leaving EV around A$440-460 million. That is only about 1.7x FY26 guided ACV of A$252-272 million after Linkurious, and likely 8-10x normalized FY26 adjusted management EBITDA if H2 follows the usual weighting. That is cheap for a mission-critical legal, regulatory, fraud, forensics, and data-governance software company that just reported 1H26 revenue up 15.2%, adjusted management EBITDA up 42.6%, free cash flow of A$20.4 million, and cash of A$57.8 million.

The most important correction to the prior thesis is that Nuix is not a clean SaaS story. Some revenue remains lumpy because multi-year software rights can still affect statutory recognition. That is exactly why ACV matters. ACV grew 8.4% to A$234.4 million in 1H26, Nuix Neo ACV grew 148% to A$46.8 million, and Neo plus Neo Discover already represented about 40% of total ACV. The company says customer migrations to Neo typically carry 30-50% ACV uplift, while new Neo sales are 2-3x the size of non-Neo sales. If that migration factory works, the inflection is not just “more revenue”, it is larger contract units, better retention, more platform breadth, and expanding margins over a cost base already funding R&D at 24% of revenue.

The variant perception is that Nuix’s “AI” angle is not promotional fluff. Generic AI plugins are weak where the customer needs evidentiary integrity, audit trails, legal defensibility, sensitive-data control, and petabyte-scale unstructured ingestion. Nuix’s pitch is more specific: use Nuix Neo as the forensic-grade governance and processing layer, then let customers bring their own AI models into controlled workflows. That matters for police, regulators, tax authorities, law firms, legal service providers, banks, and corporations that cannot just dump privileged, classified, or regulated data into a generic LLM. The March 2026 Lineal collaboration explicitly puts Neo’s AI-powered analytics at the front of eDiscovery workflows, and the older seven-year Consilio strategic deal made Consilio the first Nuix Neo Enterprise customer. Those are not tiny reference logos.

The Linkurious acquisition is also better than it first looks. Nuix paid up to €20 million for a Paris-based graph intelligence platform with about €7 million of ACV, positive EBITDA and positive operating cash flow, then updated FY26 group ACV guidance to A$252-272 million after financial close. Graph analysis is exactly what Nuix needed to move from “process the evidence” to “connect entities, events, money flows, communications, and relationships.” For financial crime, tax evasion, insider threat, cyber investigations, regulatory probes, and intelligence work, graph plus unstructured-data processing is a more valuable bundle than either tool alone.

The legal overhang is materially better but not gone. The Federal Court dismissed ASIC’s continuous-disclosure case on April 23, 2026, and ASIC itself said the court found Nuix did not breach continuous disclosure obligations or mislead investors when reaffirming forecasts after the IPO. That removes a large credibility cloud, but ASIC still had 28 days to consider appeal, and the shareholder class action trial remains scheduled for August 3, 2026. The stock should not get a clean-software multiple until the class action risk is resolved, but the direction of travel is favorable.

The operational blemish is NDR. Net dollar retention fell to 101.0% in 1H26 from 109.6%, with downsell in a few large contracts offsetting upsell elsewhere. That is the bear case in one number. If Nuix cannot lift NDR, then Neo growth is partly replacing leakage rather than compounding the base. Still, churn improved to 5.9% from 7.1%, EMEA ACV grew 18.8%, North America grew 10.8%, and APAC weakness looks more like regional execution than product irrelevance. The new CEO, John Ruthven, appointed permanent CEO on May 4, 2026, has a software résumé spanning Integrated Research, TechnologyOne, SAP, Zuora, and CA Technologies, which is the right background for a migration and go-to-market repair job.

Retail context remains useful: this is not a crowded meme. Australian retail chatter still tends to frame Nuix through the IPO disaster, CEO turnover, litigation, and chart damage. Practitioner discussion in eDiscovery is more nuanced: users still criticize pricing and Neo migration pressure, but Nuix processing is regularly treated as best-in-class for hard data problems. That gap between stock-market disgust and workflow-level product respect is the opportunity.

My base case is not that Nuix becomes a premium SaaS compounder immediately. It is that the market stops valuing it like a permanently impaired license vendor. On FY26 guidance, Nuix is roughly 1.7x EV/ACV. A defensible, cash-generative, mid-single to low-double-digit ACV grower with a fast-growing Neo segment should be worth 2.5x ACV even with litigation residue. If Neo keeps compounding, Linkurious opens financial-crime and graph-investigation cross-sell, and NDR moves back above 105-110%, 3.5-4.0x ACV is not heroic.

Targets, using current 3SJ.F price of about €0.86:

Bear: €0.55, down 36%. NDR stays around 100%, APAC weakness spreads, Linkurious integration distracts management, the class action drags on, and the market values Nuix at roughly 1.1x ACV.

Base: €1.35, up 57%. FY26 ACV lands near A$262 million, adjusted management EBITDA approaches A$50 million, free cash flow remains positive after legal and acquisition noise, and the stock rerates to about 2.5x ACV.

Bull: €2.10, up 144%. Neo becomes the majority of ACV faster than expected, NDR recovers, regulators and legal service providers standardize on AI-enabled Nuix workflows, Linkurious cross-sell works, and the market pays 3.7-4.0x ACV for a cleaned-up, cash-generative forensic AI data platform.
